items.question_text: Under 5-2-10, when may a controller instruct an aircraft to go to standby for clutter reduction, and what additional obligation applies?
items.explanation: When standby is used for clutter reduction, the controller must instruct the pilot to return to normal position as soon as possible thereafter. This obligation does not apply to the 15-mile-from-destination condition.

choices.text: When necessary to reduce clutter in a multi-target area, provided the pilot is instructed to return to normal as soon as possible thereafter.
Whenever the controller wants, as long as a code was previously assigned.
Only when the aircraft is within 15 miles of destination and you no longer desire transponder operation.
Only when the aircraft is in Class A airspace and no radar service is being provided.

items.question_text: What is the difference between the controller response when an assigned beacon code is not displayed versus when the transponder appears inoperative?
items.explanation: For a non-displaying code with an operable transponder, the controller says 'RESET TRANSPONDER, SQUAWK (code).' For an apparently inoperative transponder, the controller says 'YOUR TRANSPONDER APPEARS INOPERATIVE/MALFUNCTIONING, RESET, SQUAWK (code).' Both require notification of the subsequent control position.

choices.text: Only the inoperative case requires notifying the subsequent control position.
The non-display case uses 'RESET TRANSPONDER' without the inoperative language; the inoperative case adds 'YOUR TRANSPONDER APPEARS INOPERATIVE/MALFUNCTIONING' before the reset instruction.
The non-displaying code case requires terminating radar service immediately.
There is no operational difference; the phraseology is identical for both situations.

items.question_text: A controller disapproves a request to operate with a failed transponder because traffic is heavy. Is this permissible under 5-2-14?
items.explanation: Yes — 5-2-14 explicitly authorizes disapproving a request or withdrawing previously issued approval 'solely on the basis of traffic conditions or other operational factors.'

choices.text: No, a failed transponder request can only be disapproved if the equipment was never installed.
Yes, disapproval or withdrawal of approval may be based solely on traffic conditions or other operational factors.
No, once an approval to operate with a failed transponder is issued, it cannot be withdrawn.
Yes, but only if the pilot also agrees to file IFR.

items.question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the four trigger events requiring Mode C altitude readout validation under 5-2-15a?
items.explanation: The four triggers are: (1) initial track start, (2) track start from coast/frozen status, (3) during and after an unreliable Mode C readout, and (4) accepting an interfacility handoff (with exceptions for CTRD and ERAM facilities).

choices.text: When the pilot reports reaching the assigned altitude.
Initial track start.
Track start from coast/frozen status.
During and after an unreliable Mode C readout.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-15, what is the maximum allowable difference between the Mode C readout and pilot-reported altitude for the readout to be considered valid?
items.explanation: The readout is valid when it varies less than 300 feet from the pilot-reported altitude.

choices.text: three hundred

items.question_text: When an aircraft below FL180 has a persistently invalid Mode C readout, what two actions must the controller take under 5-2-15d2?
items.explanation: The controller must (a) instruct the pilot to turn off the altitude-reporting part of the transponder and include the reason, and (b) notify the operations supervisor-in-charge of the aircraft call sign.

choices.text: Terminate radar service and switch to nonradar procedures immediately.
Instruct the pilot to stop altitude squawk and state the reason, and notify the operations supervisor-in-charge of the aircraft call sign.
Instruct the pilot to recycle the transponder and wait 5 minutes before retesting.
Only notify the OS/CIC; no pilot instruction is required for persistent invalid readouts.

items.question_text: How does the list of exceptions for altitude confirmation differ between Mode C (5-2-16) and non-Mode C (5-2-17) aircraft?
items.explanation: Mode C has four exceptions (pilot states altitude, new altitude assigned to climbing/descending aircraft, valid Mode C readout showing established at assigned altitude, terminal intrafacility transfer). Non-Mode C has only three — it lacks the valid Mode C readout exception since there is no Mode C to read.

choices.text: Non-Mode C adds the intrafacility transfer exception that Mode C lacks.
Mode C requires confirmation on all contacts; non-Mode C never requires it.
Mode C has one additional exception: when the Mode C readout is valid and indicates the aircraft is established at the assigned altitude.
There is no difference between the two lists of exceptions.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-17b (USA), what is the controller required to do after a pilot reads back an altitude?
items.explanation: The controller must reconfirm all pilot altitude readbacks: 'AFFIRMATIVE (altitude)' if correct, or 'NEGATIVE. CLIMB/DESCEND AND MAINTAIN (altitude)' or 'NEGATIVE. MAINTAIN (altitude)' if not correct.

choices.text: Only respond if the readback is incorrect.
Acknowledge with the aircraft callsign only.
Reconfirm all pilot altitude read backs with AFFIRMATIVE or NEGATIVE as appropriate.
Reconfirmation is only required for flights above FL180.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-19, what is the prescribed sequence of actions when an inflight VFR deviation request is received from an aircraft with an inoperative transponder or Mode C between 10,000 and 18,000 feet MSL?
items.explanation: The sequence is: (1) suggest conducting the flight in airspace unaffected by the CFRs, (2) suggest filing an IFR flight plan, (3) suggest providing a VFR route of flight and maintaining radio contact with ATC. The deviation may not be approved unless the aircraft has filed IFR or provided a VFR route and maintains radio contact.

choices.text: Immediately approve all deviation requests regardless of conditions.
First suggest unaffected airspace, then IFR, then VFR route with radio contact. Do not approve unless IFR filed or VFR route provided with radio contact maintained.
First suggest the pilot squawk 1200, then approve the deviation.
Deny all inflight deviation requests without exception.

items.question_text: When an ADS-B transmitter appears inoperative, what three pieces of information must the controller provide to the OS/CIC?
items.explanation: The controller must notify the OS/CIC of the aircraft call sign, location, and time of the occurrence (UTC).

choices.text: Aircraft type, destination, and pilot name.
Transponder code, altitude, and groundspeed.
Aircraft call sign, location, and time of the occurrence (UTC).
Flight plan route, ETA, and fuel remaining.

items.question_text: A pilot with functional ADS-B Out avionics requests to turn off ADS-B transmissions. How should the controller respond under 5-2-24?
items.explanation: Controllers do not approve pilot requests for ADS-B Out OFF operations. Instead, the controller must notify the OS/CIC of the request including aircraft call sign and location. Only FAA System Operations Security can authorize such exceptions.

choices.text: Approve the request if the pilot states an operational need.
Instruct the pilot to coordinate directly with FAA System Operations Security on frequency.
Disapprove the request and notify the OS/CIC with the aircraft call sign and location.
Ignore the request since ADS-B Out must always transmit.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-24, for aircraft authorized for ADS-B Out OFF operations by FAA System Operations Security, should the controller inform them that their ADS-B transmitter appears inoperative?
items.explanation: No — 5-2-24a explicitly states 'Do not inform such aircraft that their ADS-B transmitter appears to be inoperative.' The OS/CIC should have informed the controller of the authorized OFF operations.

choices.text: Yes, inform them every time they check in on frequency.
No, do not inform such aircraft that their ADS-B transmitter appears inoperative.
Yes, but only if they ask about it.
Only if the ADS-B outage lasts more than 10 minutes.

items.instruction: Determine the correct procedure for standby operation.
items.situation: An aircraft is approximately 15 miles from its destination, and you no longer desire transponder operation. A second aircraft is in a multi-target area where clutter is degrading your ability to separate traffic.
items.question_text: What is the key operational difference between putting each of these aircraft in standby?

choices.text: Only the destination-proximity aircraft can be put in standby; clutter reduction is never a valid reason.
The clutter-reduction aircraft must be instructed to return to normal as soon as possible thereafter; the destination-proximity aircraft does not carry this return instruction obligation.
Both must be instructed to return to normal within 10 minutes.
Neither may be placed in standby; standby is only for emergency use.

items.explanation: 5-2-10B requires the 'return to normal as soon as possible thereafter' instruction specifically for the clutter-reduction case. The 15-mile-from-destination condition under 5-2-10a has no such requirement.

items.situation: You accept an interfacility handoff from a TRACON at a CTRD-equipped tower cab. The Mode C readout is displayed but you recall that interfacility handoffs require Mode C validation.
items.question_text: Must you validate the Mode C readout after this handoff?

choices.text: Yes, all interfacility handoffs require Mode C validation regardless of equipment.
Yes, but only if the readout differs by more than 300 feet.
No, CTRD-equipped tower cabs are excepted from validating Mode C after accepting interfacility handoffs from TRACONs per 5-4-3a4.
No, tower cabs never need to validate Mode C readouts.

items.explanation: 5-2-15A4(a) explicitly excepts CTRD-equipped tower cabs from the interfacility handoff validation requirement when accepting handoffs from TRACONs according to 5-4-3a4.
items.para_context: VALIDATION OF MODE C ALTITUDE READOUT

items.situation: A VFR aircraft with an inoperative transponder at 12,000 feet MSL requests a deviation from Mode C requirements. The pilot refuses to file IFR and will not provide a VFR route of flight.
items.question_text: May you approve this deviation request?

choices.text: Yes, all VFR deviation requests must be approved.
No, 5-2-19d states a deviation shall not be approved unless the aircraft has filed IFR or provided a VFR route with radio contact maintained.
Yes, if the pilot states it is an emergency.
Yes, as long as the aircraft is outside Class B airspace.

items.explanation: 5-2-19D prohibits approving an inflight deviation unless the aircraft has filed IFR or a VFR route of flight is provided and radio contact with ATC is maintained. The emergency exception (5-2-19a) applies to aircraft without transponder equipment installed, not inoperative equipment.
items.para_context: INFLIGHT DEVIATIONS FROM TRANSPONDER/MODE C REQUIREMENTS

items.instruction: Determine the correct response to ADS-B malfunction.
items.situation: A civil aircraft's ADS-B transmitter appears to be malfunctioning and the malfunction is jeopardizing the safe execution of air traffic control functions.

choices.text: Only notify the OS/CIC and continue normal operations.
Instruct the aircraft to stop ADS-B transmissions and notify the OS/CIC.
Terminate radar service for that aircraft immediately.
Instruct the aircraft to squawk standby and continue on primary radar only.

items.explanation: 5-2-22B: when a malfunctioning ADS-B transmitter is jeopardizing the safe execution of ATC functions, the controller must instruct the aircraft to stop ADS-B transmissions and notify the OS/CIC. The phraseology is 'STOP ADS-B TRANSMISSIONS, AND IF ABLE, SQUAWK THREE/ALFA (code).'
